title: What is a struct
instruction: Imagine a library catalog card. One card holds a book's title, its author, its ISBN — all related, all together.\nA struct is exactly that. It is a custom type that groups related fields under one name.\nRun this. A `Book` struct is defined with two fields: `Title` and `Pages`. Then we create one and print it.
successMessage: One variable, two pieces of information. That is a struct. Notice Go prints it as {The Go Programming Language 400} — all fields together in curly braces.

title: Define a struct
instruction: Define an `Author` struct with three fields: `Name` (string), `Nationality` (string), and `BirthYear` (int). Then create one with your favourite author and print it.
hint: type Author struct { Name string; Nationality string; BirthYear int } — then a := Author{Name: "Margaret Atwood", Nationality: "Canadian", BirthYear: 1939} and fmt.Println(a)

codeChecks.message: Define a struct called Author: type Author struct { ... }
Add a Name string field to the struct.
Add a Nationality string field to the struct.
Add a BirthYear int field to the struct.
Create an Author value: a := Author{Name: ..., Nationality: ..., BirthYear: ...}
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Your own custom type — defined, created, and printed. Every real Go application is full of types like this.

title: achha — dot notation accesses fields
instruction: To read a specific field, use a dot: `book.Title`, `book.Pages`. You don't have to print the whole struct at once.\nPrint `Title` and `Pages` on separate lines using dot notation.

codeChecks.message: Access the Title field: b.Title
Access the Pages field: b.Pages
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Dot notation is how you access every field in Go. Clean, readable, no guessing.

title: Update a field
instruction: Fields work like variables. You can reassign them after creation.\n`b.Pages = 350` updates the Pages field directly.\nUpdate `member.Active` to `true` and `member.FineBalance` to 0.0, then print the whole struct.

codeChecks.message: Update Active: member.Active = true
Update FineBalance: member.FineBalance = 0.0
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Same struct, updated fields. Notice the name stayed as Alice because we only changed Active and FineBalance.

title: Zero values in structs
instruction: When you declare a struct without setting all fields, Go fills the rest with zero values — empty string for strings, 0 for ints, false for bools.\nRun this and see what an unfilled struct looks like.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

type Book struct {
	Title   string
	Pages   int
	InStock bool
	Rating  float64
}

func main() {
	var b Book
	fmt.Println(b.Title)
	fmt.Println(b.Pages)
	fmt.Println(b.InStock)
	fmt.Println(b.Rating)
}
successMessage: Empty string, zero int, false bool, zero float. Every field gets its type's zero value. Safe and predictable — Go never leaves memory uninitialised.

title: Struct as function parameter and return value
instruction: You can pass structs to functions and return them. When you pass a struct normally, Go makes a copy.\nCall `printBook` with your favourite book. Then add a `newAuthor` function that takes a name and nationality and returns an `Author`, call it, and print the result.
hint: printBook(Book{Title: "Dune", Pages: 688})  func newAuthor(name string, nationality string) Author { return Author{Name: name, Nationality: nationality} }

codeChecks.message: Call printBook() with a Book value.
Define a newAuthor function that returns an Author.
Return an Author struct from newAuthor.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Structs flow through functions just like ints and strings. Constructor pattern — one function, clean creation.

title: Pointer to a struct (avoid copies)
instruction: When you pass a struct normally, Go copies it. Use a pointer to pass the original.\n`func addPages(b *Book) { b.Pages += 50 }` — the `*` means it receives the address, not a copy.\nCreate a Book pointer, call `addPages`, and see that Pages actually changed.
hint: b := &Book{Title: "Go in Action", Pages: 300}  addPages(b)  fmt.Println(b.Pages)

codeChecks.message: Create a pointer to a Book: b := &Book{...}
Call addPages(b) with the pointer.
Print b.Pages after calling addPages.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: The page count went up because addPages received a pointer — not a copy. The & creates a pointer, the * in the parameter says 'this is a pointer, modify the original'.

title: Nested structs
instruction: A struct can contain another struct as a field. A `Book` has an `Author`.\nRun this, then update `b.Author.Nationality` to "British" and print the full author.

codeChecks.message: Update b.Author.Nationality to "British"
Print the full b.Author.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Nested fields accessed with dot-dot notation. Real data is rarely flat — nested structs let you model it naturally.

title: Anonymous struct
instruction: Sometimes you need a struct for one specific thing and don't need to reuse it. An anonymous struct has no type name — defined and used inline.\n`book := struct { Title string; Pages int }{Title: "Brave New World", Pages: 311}`\nRun this. Notice there is no `type Book struct` declaration anywhere.
successMessage: No type declaration needed. Anonymous structs are great for one-off groupings — shelf labels, config values, small response shapes.

title: Slice of structs
instruction: In real programs you rarely have just one struct. You have a list of them — a slice of structs.\n`books := []Book{{...}, {...}, {...}}`\nLoop through the slice and print each book's title and page count.
hint: for _, b := range books { fmt.Printf("%s has %d pages\n", b.Title, b.Pages) }

codeChecks.message: Loop through books with range: for _, b := range books
Access b.Title inside the loop.
Access b.Pages inside the loop.
Replace the TODO comment with your loop.

successMessage: Slice of structs — the backbone of almost every Go application. Book lists, author catalogs, member directories.

title: Struct tags (JSON serialization)
instruction: Struct tags are metadata attached to fields. They tell encoding libraries how to handle each field.\n`type Book struct { Title string \`json:"title"\`; Pages int \`json:"pages"\` }`\nThe tag tells Go's JSON encoder to use lowercase keys. Run this and see the JSON output.
successMessage: The Go struct with capital letters became lowercase JSON keys. Struct tags control serialization — JSON, XML, database column names, form parsing.

title: Build a library catalog
instruction:
No starter code. Build a library catalog from scratch:
1. Define an `Author` struct with `Name` (string), `Nationality` (string)
2. Define a `Book` struct with `Title` (string), `Pages` (int), `Author` (Author)
3. Create a `[]Book` slice with at least 3 books
4. Loop through and print each book: `Title: Dune | Pages: 688 | Author: Frank Herbert (American)`
hint: Use nested struct access: b.Author.Name, b.Author.Nationality inside a loop.

codeChecks.message: Define an Author struct.
Define a Book struct.
Add an Author field (of type Author) to Book.
Create a slice of Books: books := []Book{...}
Loop through books with range.

successMessage: A complete library catalog. Custom types, nested struct, slice, loop — all the pieces working together. This is how Go programs are built in the real world.
